sentences.text: Yeah.
[ PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
I was in school till 1936, as I said.
And the Nuremberg laws were in 1935, the year before.
And many things happened.
And we felt that they were going worse.
Because I think about that time in '35 or '36, Hitler made an agreement with Mussolini.
And that made it much worse.
And in school I remained the only Jewish person in the school of about 700 children.
When-- when we came into the school, you had to raise your hand.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The teacher knew that I was Jewish.
And as I said before, since he was an older teacher, and he knew a little bit about life, so he did not treat me any different.
Even though in the very beginning, you see, they was so much for me that they even wanted to elect me as a class president.
But I didn't want to.
But as time went on, and most of the youngsters were in the Hitler Youth, Hitlerjugend.
And they were-- there were spies all around.
In every block there was a spy, a Nazi spy, who found out if anyone was 100% behind Hitler, or just was trying to-- to make up as he-- as he would, as he was.
So therefore, they started to stay away from the Jews as much as possible.
In the beginning, not much different.
But then afterwards, when I went to the oberrealschule, which are last three classes in school, one day we had to write an essay class essay.
And this class essay was about German loyalty to the leader, category [NON-ENGLISH].
And I have a copy of this essay.
It was written in a group.
And among these people there was one, especially one student, he had a French name.
And he was very antisemitic.
And we wrote the essay, and this essay was about the loyalty, as I said.
And in this essay we wrote that we are the only true ones in this world, the Nazis, you see.
We actually are the ones [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] who are the strongest.
And we will conquer the world.
And we will conquer even the United States.
And why will you conquer the United States?
So they wrote in this essay, because in the United States they have fighting between the races, black and white.
They will kill each other, and in the end we will take over.
I mean they wrote all this in the essay.
I didn't believe it, you see.

And then finally when the school was nearly at its end, and we made the final exam, and the result of the final exam is called Abitur, A-B-I-T-U-R, which is an entrance exam to a university, everyone had to write an essay what they would do afterwards.
I wrote, and I told the teacher before, actually this goes back in a way, beginning when the teacher said Jews cannot go on Saturday anymore to school.
So he asked me, why don't you want to go now?
So I said to him, I tell you.
Since Hitler came to power, the Germans want to be more German 'Nazi' in quotation mark, and I want to be more Jewish.
And then afterwards at this essay at the end of the school year, I wrote down.
I still have the outline that's written in German that since there is nothing left over for me in Germany what to do, I cannot do anything else.
I would study to be a Jewish teacher maybe.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that was it.
The exam at that time was three days.
And three days of-- of tests, especially history.
You know they study lot of history, much more than here in the United States, and also languages.
I took Latin, and I had a little bit of Spanish, and a little bit of English, and a little bit of French.
Once I finished school there, I went to the Jewish community.
And they were working with Jews to emigrate, or those who wanted to be there, they could only go to a Jewish institution.

The only Jewish institution left there was a teacher seminary that was in Wurzburg, which is about 400 miles-- 400 miles-- maybe-- I don't know if it's miles or kilometers, but in southern Germany, in Bavaria.
And he got me a scholarship there, and I went there.
I went there from 1936-- wait a second-- to 19-- no, I forget.
I'm sorry.
[LAUGHS] I first-- I first went to Frankfurt Am Main, to the yeshiva in Frankfurt Am Main.
The Teacher seminary came later.
From 1936 to 1937, and after that I went to Wurzburg in 1937.
In Frankfurt, that was a bigger city than Bremen.
I went there.
I didn't know very much at that time.
I hadn't learned very much in-- in Jewish subjects.
But in a short while, I went from the lowest to the highest class.
And I stayed there for one year.
It was like one year army service.
You see they want you one year.
Afterwards like-- like they do now in the United States, when they finish the school, they go for one year to Israel.
And then they come back.
So I'm there for one year, and then I went to the Wurzburg Seminary.
I lived more or less in the protected environment.
Because I was in the school.
I lived in the dormitory.
But I know that many, many things were going outside.
And Wurzburg was the capital of Bavaria-- in-- in Franconia.
They had a king there at one time, Otto, King Otto, a big residence.
And we pass by there every day.
They didn't have a king, of course, at that time.
Because that was all abolished in 1918, where all the kingdoms in Europe were abolished, except for a few.
So I spent most of my time in the seminary, in Wurzburg, or in the dormitory.
So I didn't see much from the outside.
I did see from the outside trains passing by near the seminary that were covered with tarp and with-- and there were machine guns under there, and war materials.
They send it every day.
Because it was on their way, the Germans were on their way to conquer Czechoslovakia, and to conquer Austria.
So they had this whole center.
And they wanted you to believe it didn't exist.
And in the streets, the non-Jewish children, they sometimes attacked me, took away my hat, and threw it away, things like that.
Yeah.
I went home during the summer.
But you have to know that during the war-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] yeah, I went home.
But I needed always permission to travel in Germany.
I needed permission to travel, let's say, from Bremen to Frankfurt, from Bremen to-- to Wurzburg.
And there are stamps in my passport.
No, it was not too difficult, just we had to get it.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
In the seminary, it was from March, the school year in Germany starts in March, about Passover time, about Easter time, from March to March.
I was there from 1937 March till the Kristallnacht when it was closed by force.
Till the 9th of November, 1938.
I was just about finishing up.
Because when I came to Wurzburg, because of my Abitur, I only had to go there two years to get a teacher's diploma.
If you didn't go, otherwise you would have to go six years.
So I went to these two years in a special course, which they called a transitional course.
So I stayed there till the end, till it was closed.
Did you get your certificate?
I was in the middle of my tests when it was closed.
But somehow, we got a certificate afterwards, because a director who also, whom they took also doing that night and send him to a concentration camp.
Afterwards, he made some kind arrangements that we got this certificate, even I still have it.
The Nazi Party gave a certificate that this was a recognized school.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
It was very nice.
We had trains.
It was a big building.
We got food there.
We had a gym.
And the people there came from all parts of Germany.
And just to study there, you see.
Younger ones, with some of them I still have correspondence now, some of them are in Buenos Aires, Argentina.
And I still have correspondence with him.
And besides this, the people from that institute about 20 years ago in 1976, they started to have biannual reunions.
We had one last summer.
Of course, the number goes down.
Because people get older.
People die.
People get sick and they can't travel anymore.
[LAUGHS] So it goes down.
But we still remember this.
Because it-- it had a great influence on many of these people.
I'm still the editor of this newsletter.
That was the worst thing in the world.
I said to myself at that time, from now on things will never be the same again, never.
And I remember that we studied in school about Goethe, who was a great German poet.
And he said this about the French Revolution in 1789.
He said in 1789, from now on, the world will be all different.
I said, from this day on, the world will be all different.
No one will ever forget it, not even a child in the crib will forget what happened on Kristallnacht, if they were there.
Because during the day before, on the 9th of November, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I went to school to study for the test, which was supposed to be the next day.
And we studied very much.
Suddenly, a boy came in.
This boy was from Kassel.
Of course now he isn't a boy anymore.
That was 60 years ago, or 70 years ago.
And he said, he said, I got a call from Kassel that they are going to burn the synagogue in Kassel.
Kassel is a German city.
And every hour some new things came in.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But actually it did not start at Kristallnacht.
It started actually three days before, actually more than that.
Because a few days before, our school, I mean the teacher's seminary where I was, would make an annual excursion.
And we took an excursion to a little place called Kitzingen.
And we went there.
And when we went to this little village, which is only a few streets, you see in Germany they have like a kiosk, and they put all the announcements on the kiosk.
I saw a kiosk there that-- against the Jews, very much so.
Many people were standing around.
And-- and there was an announcement that a Jewish boy had killed the German ligate in Paris.
His name was Herschel Grynszpan, and I think I remember him.
Because he used to go to the Shiva in Frankfurt.
And he came from a village not far from Bremen, [?
Hannover. ?] So he had attacked him.
And we must take revenge, it said on this bulletin.
So I was very much afraid something bad would happen.
And it did happen then actually in the night of the-- from the 9th through the 10th, actually before.
On the 9th it happened.
My school happened like this.
In the evening, making a long story short, in the evening I went home.
Because I wanted to rest well for the test on the next day.
I came home.
To the dormitory, my home in Wurzburg.
Excuse me.
And I went there.
And went to bed early.
And I wanted to sleep well for the next night, and so forth.
But a little later, two of my roommates-- there were three people in that room-- came in.
And they also went to sleep.
And we were woken-- we were woken up, awakened about, let's say, 3 o'clock in the morning.
There was a big noise, a big bang, bang, crash, bam, bang.
And they broke into this building.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't want to go out.
Because I thought it was dangerous.
So I stayed in the room.
The room had the only way, a little lock, a small lock.
Anyone could easily break it.
But we locked it.
And we-- we stayed there.
And we said, let's wait and see what's going to happen.
Now, this room where I was in, they had a big dormitory.
And we were in a side room, in a small room.
There were only three beds in that room.
And it had on the top a small window on the top.
It's barricaded.
It must have been an army building at one time.
I don't know.
[LAUGHS]
So, we were in there.
And we waited.
Then the noise disappeared.
After a certain, half an hour maybe again, no-- a new noise came into the building.
And I heard voices, people coming.
And they came closer to us, and closer and closer.
Then I said, the best thing for us to do in such a situation, let's get ready, dress ourselves, get dressed.
I said-- I woke the others up.
And I said, let's get dressed.
And then we were in a better position, you see, to confront them, and see what's going to happen.
It's no-- no-- no use to hide.
We tried to hide behind the commode there, and so no use.
And pack your things.
We packed our things.
We didn't have much.
But we packed it.
And really this happened, after a little while, he just broke into the door.
And he came in.
And we stood up.
And-- and it wasn't-- it was a person in civilian uniform, you see.

Because the Nazis, during that day, did not want to show that it is the Nazi party that actually was behind it, but the [? volkswut, ?] which means the-- the anger-- the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the anger of the people-- the people.
But he was a Nazi.
And he said to us, this time we are not going to break a hair, we do anything to you.
But next time that will be the end.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So, what should we do?
So he said to us, here, you have your packages there.
Take them.
Throw them against the window.
Of course it didn't break.
Because there were iron-- bars in there.
But it got stuck in there, you see.
Then he said, what else?
There were typewriters in the [INAUDIBLE], throw them through out there to the-- to the yard.
Then they said to us, go downstairs.
He said to us.
And we went downstairs.
Now we passed by the big dormitory, and there were water fountains.
And they had hacked them off so that the water just came out.
There were lamps hanging there.
They just cut them off, you see, with axes and so forth, what he came with.
They broke the whole building.
We came downstairs.
Downstairs there was living a Jewish janitor, the couple.
And they made coffee for us, and some other things.
And the man, said maybe I call the police.
So he went over to the police.
He never came back, because the police were told, do nothing during the night.
Be passive.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Then there was also a-- a gentile person there.
And he was a very nice person.
So we asked him, what should we do?
He said to them, he said to us, get ready.
Pack your things.
Because I think they will all take you and bring you to a prison.
So-- so we did this.
We waited downstairs from about 3:00, 4:30 or so, till about 6:00, 7:00 in the morning.
And then at 6 o'clock or 6:30 in the morning, there came in a lot of people again.
And some of the leaders-- I don't even think they had a leader-- said to us, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] make lines of five or six in a line, ready to march.
We asked, should we take anything along?
You don't have to take anything along.
Like they always did.
You see when someone went to-- afterwards to concentration, you don't have to take anything along.
They knew they would not get it anyhow.
So we didn't take very many things along.
And we went in lines.
There were all boys around my age, 14, 15, 16 years of age.
And-- and we formed lines.
And then we started to walk.
They said, walk.
It was a very small street.
[INAUDIBLE] Strasse is the name of that place.
And outside they told all the-- all the other people, stay outside at the side and watch it.
It's a spectacle.
[LAUGHS]
So-- so they went outside, and we walked in front.
And they spit at us, and they called names, like animals.
So we walked through the street to a small street, and we came by.
On the right-hand side, not far, there was a synagogue.
We saw the synagogue was burning.
And we went past this big place, the-- the King's Palace, which was there.
And they bought us to a prison, the Wurzburg Prison.
And when we came to the prison they told us, wait here, we have to register you.
And what they did with the Jews when they send them to prison and afterwards to concentration camp, they photographed them from three sides.
They want to find out.
Do they have this hook nose?
Do they have these-- nose-- these ears which are detached?
[LAUGHS] Do they have this skull, you see.
Do they have these kind of ears, of eyes, all kind of things?
They took all kinds of measurements.
They photographed us from three sides.
Then they say, take everything out of your pockets.
And they were very organized.
That I must say.
The Nazis were very organized.
They wrote down every little thing.
That's why we have so many names now of people in concentration camps.
Otherwise we wouldn't have any names.
So they wrote down you had a watch.
You had pot money.
Pot money means your-- Wallet?
--purse a purse.
You had so much money in there, you see.
And these things all that they put down.
And they put it in, and they left in their safe there or something like this.
Then they said, now go upstairs.
And they brought us into different cells.
Me, they brought into cell number 99.
I thought it was a good name, a good number, you see, 99.
[LAUGHS] And there were about 15 boys in this cell.
It was a small room, maybe like this size, or maybe smaller.
And-- and there was only a window on top.
And there was only a wooden table and wooden planks, no mattresses actually.
In-- in the door there was a window.
And through this window they handed out some food sometimes.
But the food was just water with a few beans in it.
But I tried to keep up a positive note in this, in this.
I was not the oldest, actually.
And I said to them, OK.
Let's pray.
We told jokes to each other, you see.
And we sang a little bit.
And I had a pair of tefillin with me.
You what that is?
And we all used it.
I was able to hide it somehow.
That was the thing.
Do you want to hear more about it?
Mm-hmm.
OK.
So this went on.
I was there for eight days in the Wurzburg Prison.
Now every day in the morning, they fed the prisoners walk for one hour to keep us strong, you know?
And then in the afternoon, they called out a number of names from a list on a yellow sheet of paper.
And these people came out.
The next day, we didn't see these people anymore.
Because they were shipped off to concentration camps.
Then comes day number seven.
That was the Thursday after.
It was probably-- let me see-- 10, that was the 17th of November.
Then at noontime, they came at noontime.
They had sometimes two appeals they call them, appell in German.
And they write down names.
So they write down seven names, and they said you stand here.
And I was among the seven people whom they called up there.
Then after the others had left, they said to us, you are not German citizens.
So we will send you back to the land of your origin.
I said, so where do I go I?
I'm stateless.
So they said, you go back to Russia.
Because your father came from Russia.
And in Germany, the citizenship goes after the father.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I said, OK.
Better in Russia than here, even though Stalin was just as bad as anyone else, as Hitler.
So we went.
And I thought that really during the night when I slept, I said to myself, now next-- tomorrow I'll be in Russia.
I thought about it.
But things changed.
Because next morning what happened is, during the night they sent all the others to concentration camps.
They wanted to empty the prison, to empty it out.
So all the others went to concentration camps.
And in the next day, during the morning, they called again the seven people together.
And it was in the evening.
It was in the evening.
They said to our seven, you are free.
Go downstairs to the office and get your papers.
